subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 16! In this unit you will discover the hidden rules that keep all of mathematics fair, consistent and reliable. These rules are called the laws of arithmetic, and they decide the order in which we carry out calculations and how we are allowed to rearrange numbers to make our work easier. Just as the traffic in Mogadishu only flows safely when every driver follows the same rules of the road, a calculation only gives one trustworthy answer when everyone follows the same rules of arithmetic.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Apply the order of operations (BODMAS / BIDMAS) to evaluate expressions with several operations.
Use brackets, including nested brackets, to control the order of a calculation.
Apply the commutative law for addition and multiplication, and explain why subtraction and division are not commutative.
Apply the associative law for addition and multiplication to group numbers efficiently.
Apply the distributive law, a × (b + c) = (a × b) + (a × c), and use it in reverse to factor out a common number.
Choose the smartest law to simplify a calculation and do more of it in your head.
Translate word problems into expressions and check answers using a second method and estimation.

concepts.title: The Order of Operations (BODMAS)
concepts.explanation: B – Brackets (also called parentheses) are always done first. O – Orders (powers and roots, also called indices) come next.
concepts.example: DM – Division and Multiplication, worked from left to right.

concepts.title: The Power of Brackets
concepts.explanation: Brackets are the most powerful symbols in an expression because they let us override the standard order of operations. Whatever sits inside a pair of brackets must be worked out first, no matter what operations surround it. In this way brackets act like a traffic officer at a busy Nairobi junction, waving one calculation through ahead of all the others. Look at how brackets can completely change a result. Without brackets, 3 + 4 × 2 follows BODMAS: the multiplication first, 3 + 8 = 11. But with brackets, (3 + 4) ×…
concepts.example: Brackets are not only about changing the order – they can also make a calculation easier even when they do not change the answer. Suppose Farah must work out 25 × 4 + 25 × 6. She could plod through it as 100 + 150 =…

concepts.title: The Commutative Law
concepts.explanation: The commutative law is the most intuitive of the three great laws. It says that for addition and multiplication, the order of the numbers does not change the answer. In symbols, for any two numbers a and b: Addition: a + b = b + a, for example 7 + 3 = 3 + 7 = 10.
concepts.example: Multiplication: a × b = b × a, for example 6 × 4 = 4 × 6 = 24.

concepts.title: The Associative Law
concepts.explanation: The associative law is about grouping. It says that when three or more numbers are all added, or all multiplied, the way we group them with brackets does not change the answer. In symbols, for any three numbers a, b and c: Addition: (a + b) + c = a + (b + c). For example (2 + 3) + 4 = 5 + 4 = 9 and 2 + (3 + 4) = 2 + 7 = 9.
concepts.example: Multiplication: (a × b) × c = a × (b × c). For example (2 × 3) × 4 = 6 × 4 = 24 and 2 × (3 × 4) = 2 × 12 = 24.

concepts.title: The Distributive Law
concepts.explanation: The distributive law is the most powerful law of all, because it links multiplication with addition and subtraction. It tells us that multiplying a number by a sum is the same as multiplying by each part and then adding. In symbols, for any three numbers a, b and c: Let us check that it really works. 5 × (10 + 3): the direct route is 5 × 13 = 65; the distributive route is 5 × 10 + 5 × 3 = 50 + 15 = 65. They match. 7 × (20 − 2): direct route 7 × 18 = 126; distributive route 7 × 20 − 7 × 2 = 140 − 14 = 126. They…
concepts.example: Why does it work? Picture a rectangle, the way land is measured for a farm outside Garissa. Suppose it is 5 metres wide and (10 + 3) metres long. You can find the area in one piece as 5 × 13 = 65 square metres, or you…

concepts.title: Using the Laws Together to Simplify
concepts.explanation: The real power comes when you combine the order of operations with all three laws to make a hard calculation simple. A good mathematician looks at an expression before diving in and asks, ‘Is there a smarter path?’ Very often there is. When you meet a complex expression, follow this systematic plan: first identify all the brackets and operations; then apply BODMAS carefully; then look for chances to use the commutative, associative or distributive laws to simplify; work step by step, writing each stage clearly;…
concepts.example: Example: 3 × (4 + 2) − 5 × (7 − 3). Brackets first: 3 × 6 − 5 × 4. Multiplication next: 18 − 20. Subtraction last: 18 − 20 = −2. Notice the answer is a negative number, which is perfectly allowed.

methods.title: How to evaluate an expression
steps: Do everything inside brackets first (innermost first if nested).
Work out any orders (powers and roots).
Do division and multiplication, left to right.
Do addition and subtraction, left to right.
Check your answer with a second method if you can.

methods.title: How to use the distributive law for mental maths
steps: Split the awkward number into an easy sum or difference, such as 47 = 40 + 7 or 99 = 100 − 1.
Multiply the outside number by each part.
Add or subtract the two results.

methods.title: How to factor out a common number
steps: Look for the same number multiplying two or more terms.
Write that number outside a bracket.
Put the remaining parts inside the bracket, then simplify.

methods.title: Using the commutative and associative laws
steps: Solution: Rearrange the order (commutative) and regroup (associative) to make a round number: 2 × 5 × 39 = (2 × 5) × 39 = 10 × 39 = 390.
Multiplying by 10 is far easier than multiplying by 39 directly.
Check the using the commutative and associative laws result carefully.

methods.title: Distributive law for mental maths
methods.example: Work out 8 × 34 without a written algorithm.
steps: Solution: Split 34 into 30 + 4 and distribute: 8 × 34 = 8 × 30 + 8 × 4 = 240 + 32 = 272.
Check the distributive law for mental maths result carefully.
Check the distributive law for mental maths result carefully.

methods.title: Factoring out a common number
steps: Solution: Every term shares the factor 15, so pull it out: 15 × (7 + 3 + 10) = 15 × 20 = 300.
Factoring turns three multiplications into one.
Check the factoring out a common number result carefully.

workedExamples.title: A three-operation BODMAS sum
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The multiplication is the strongest operation here, so do it first: 4 × 3 = 12, giving 20 − 12 + 7. Now work the addition and subtraction left to right: 20 − 12 = 8, then 8 + 7 = 15.

workedExamples.title: Division and multiplication together
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Division and multiplication sit at the same level, so complete both before adding. 36 ÷ 9 = 4 and 5 × 2 = 10, giving 4 + 10 = 14.

workedExamples.title: Nested brackets
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start with the innermost bracket: 2 + 3 = 5, giving 4 × (10 − 5). Next the remaining bracket: 10 − 5 = 5, giving 4 × 5. Finally 4 × 5 = 20.

workedExamples.title: Using the commutative and associative laws
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rearrange the order (commutative) and regroup (associative) to make a round number: 2 × 5 × 39 = (2 × 5) × 39 = 10 × 39 = 390. Multiplying by 10 is far easier than multiplying by 39 directly.

workedExamples.title: Distributive law for mental maths
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 8 × 34 without a written algorithm.

workedExamples.title: Factoring out a common number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Every term shares the factor 15, so pull it out: 15 × (7 + 3 + 10) = 15 × 20 = 300. Factoring turns three multiplications into one.

workedExamples.title: A mixed expression with brackets
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Brackets first: 5 × 8 − 3 × 4. Multiplication next: 40 − 12. Subtraction last: 40 − 12 = 28.

workedExamples.title: Translating words with brackets
workedExamples.prompt: Halima thinks of a number, adds 6, then multiplies the result by 4. Write this as an expression and work it out if her number is 7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: ‘Adds 6, then multiplies the result by 4’ means the whole sum must be multiplied, so we need brackets: (n + 6) × 4. With n = 7 this is (7 + 6) × 4 = 13 × 4 = 52. Without the brackets, 7 + 6 × 4 would wrongly give 31, a different answer entirely.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Use the distributive law to work out in your head: a) 6 × 23 b) 8 × 45 c) 9 × 99.

workedExamples.title: Factor out the common number
workedExamples.prompt: Factor out the common number: a) 4 × 13 + 4 × 7 b) 9 × 6 + 9 × 4.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Place one pair of brackets in 12 − 4 × 2 so that the answer becomes 16.

activities.title: The BODMAS Card Challenge
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and small cards or scraps of paper.
steps: Write the numbers 1 to 9 on separate cards, and write the four operation signs +, −, ×, ÷ and a pair of brackets on other cards.
Deal yourself three number cards and two operation cards, then build an expression.
Solve it carefully using BODMAS, writing each step.
Now add a pair of brackets somewhere and see how the answer changes. Can you make the answer as large as possible? As small as possible?
This game trains you to see how the order of operations and brackets shape every result.

activities.title: The Rectangle Area Investigation (Distributive Law)
activities.materials: squared paper (or draw your own grid), a pencil and a ruler.
steps: Draw a rectangle 6 squares tall and 13 squares wide. Count or calculate its area: 6 × 13.
Now draw a line to split the width 13 into 10 + 3, making two smaller rectangles.
Find the area of each smaller rectangle: 6 × 10 and 6 × 3.
Add the two areas and check they match your first answer. You have just proved 6 × 13 = 6 × 10 + 6 × 3!
Try it again with a different rectangle, such as 8 × 12 split into 8 × 10 + 8 × 2.

activities.title: Mental Maths Speed Game
activities.materials: a family member or friend, and a timer or clock.
steps: One player calls out a multiplication like 6 × 48 or 7 × 99.
The other player must solve it out loud using the distributive law (for example, 6 × 48 = 6 × 50 − 6 × 2).
Score a point for each correct answer solved this way within ten seconds.
Swap roles after five turns. The player with more points wins the round.
This game shows how the laws of arithmetic turn hard sums into quick mental ones.

activities.title: Grouping with Market Stones (Associative Law)
activities.materials: a big handful of stones, beans or bottle caps to act as ‘shillings’.
steps: Set out three piles, for example 25, 17 and 75 stones.
Add them by grouping the two that make a round number first: (25 + 75) + 17.
Now add them in a different grouping, such as 25 + (17 + 75), and check you get the same total.
Talk about why the grouping did not change the answer – this is the associative law of addition.

activities.title: Bracket Detective (Family Puzzle)
activities.materials: paper and pencil for each player.
steps: Write an expression with no brackets, such as 2 + 3 × 4 − 1.
Challenge your family to place one pair of brackets to reach a target answer you choose (for example, make it equal 19).
Everyone shows their bracket placement and checks it with BODMAS.
Take turns being the puzzle-setter. The more surprising the target, the better!

activities.title: The Laws Poster Investigation
activities.materials: a large sheet of paper and coloured pens.
steps: Divide your poster into four boxes: Order of Operations, Commutative Law, Associative Law, Distributive Law.
In each box, write the rule in your own words and draw a clear example.
Add one real-life picture for each law, such as a rectangle field for the distributive law.
Show your poster to your teacher and explain each law aloud. Teaching it proves you truly understand it.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which law was the most surprising to discover? Which activity made the distributive law clearest for you? Where in everyday life did you notice one of these laws at work? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: The Order of Operations (BODMAS) B – Brackets first. O – Orders (powers and roots) next. DM – Division and Multiplication, left to right. AS – Addition and Subtraction, left to right.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Commutative Law Addition: a + b = b + a, for example 7 + 3 = 3 + 7. Multiplication: a × b = b × a, for example 6 × 4 = 4 × 6. It does NOT work for subtraction or division.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Associative Law Addition: (a + b) + c = a + (b + c). Multiplication: (a × b) × c = a × (b × c). It is about grouping, and only works for addition or multiplication.

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: Distributive Law a × (b + c) = (a × b) + (a × c). a × (b − c) = (a × b) − (a × c). In reverse it lets you factor out a shared number.

terms: Symbols ( ) that are worked out first
Order does not matter (+ and × only)
Grouping does not matter (+ and × only)
a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c
The distributive law used in reverse
A single number or product in an expression

questions.question: Calculate 2 × 39 × 5 the smart way.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 16 methods.
questions.explanation: Rearrange the order (commutative) and regroup (associative) to make a round number: 2 × 5 × 39 = (2 × 5) × 39 = 10 × 39 = 390. Multiplying by 10 is far easier than multiplying by 39 directly.

selfAssessment: I can apply the order of operations (BODMAS / BIDMAS) to evaluate expressions with several operations.
I can use brackets, including nested brackets, to control the order of a calculation.
I can apply the commutative law for addition and multiplication, and explain why subtraction and division are not commutative.
I can apply the associative law for addition and multiplication to group numbers efficiently.
I can apply the distributive law, a × (b + c) = (a × b) + (a × c), and use it in reverse to factor out a common number.
I can choose the smartest law to simplify a calculation and do more of it in your head.
I can translate word problems into expressions and check answers using a second method and estimation.
